title: Automated IEEE Paper Generation Using docx-js and Node.js
authors: John Doe, Jane Smith, and Robert Brown
affiliation: Department of Computer Science, Innovative University, Thailand
abstract: This research explores the automation of academic paper formatting using the docx-js library. By mapping IEEE editorial standards to programmatic rules, we reduce formatting errors and save time for researchers.
keywords: IEEE Standards, Automation, docx-js, Academic Writing, Node.js

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The introduction provides background on the importance of standardized formatting in academic publishing. IEEE style is widely recognized but can be challenging to implement manually.

sections.title: Related Work
sections.content: Previous tools like LaTeX provide high-quality output but have a steep learning curve. Our approach uses JavaScript to provide a familiar environment for web developers.

subsections.title: Layout Engine Design
subsections.content: The engine uses a section-based approach to handle the transition from single-column titles to double-column body text.

subsections.title: Font and Style Mapping
subsections.content: Every font size is converted to docx-compatible half-points (e.g., 10pt = 20 units).

sections.title: Results and Discussion
sections.content: Our tests show that the generated documents align perfectly with the IEEE Word template used in major conferences.

sections.title: Conclusion
sections.content: Automation in academic writing is feasible and highly effective. Future versions will support automatic BibTeX integration.

acknowledgment: This work was supported by the Open Source Development Grant #2026.
references: [1] G. O. Young, “Synthetic structure of industrial plastics,” in Plastics, 2nd ed., vol. 3, J. Peters, Ed. New York: McGraw-Hill, 1964, pp. 15–64.
[2] W.-K. Chen, Linear Networks and Systems. Belmont, CA: Wadsworth, 1993, pp. 123–135.
